items.title: Experts still beat Claude Code users
items.oneliner: Anthropic says users make 70% of plans while Claude executes 80%; tooling is improving faster than operator judgment.

items.title: Snap shipped expensive AI AR glasses
items.oneliner: Snap's $2,195 Specs put AI agents on your face, but the real test is whether developers build useful loops.

items.title: US export controls hit frontier models
items.oneliner: Anthropic's top models reportedly went offline under export restrictions, showing geopolitics can now break your product roadmap.

items.title: GitHub hosted a massive malware cluster
items.oneliner: A researcher found 10,000 GitHub repos pushing Trojans, which makes dependency trust even shakier for every engineering team.

items.title: AMD quietly dropped Ryzen memory encryption
items.oneliner: AMD removed consumer Ryzen memory encryption in newer firmware without clear disclosure, a bad precedent for hardware security assumptions.

items.title: DeepSeek avoided a US blacklist
items.oneliner: The US paused blacklisting DeepSeek despite flagging 100-plus firms, leaving AI buyers stuck between access and compliance risk.

items.title: Firecracker made cloud browsers nearly instant
items.oneliner: Browser sessions now start in under one second at $0.02 per hour, which resets expectations for agent browser infrastructure.

items.title: Elastic published an agent memory stack
items.oneliner: Elastic says its Elasticsearch memory layer hit 0.89 recall, making long-lived agents feel more like systems than demos.

items.title: Epic open sourced a binary-first VCS
items.oneliner: Lore targets huge binary assets with content-addressed versioning, which could matter anywhere Git still chokes on scale.

items.title: Sogen shipped a syscall-level emulator
items.oneliner: Sogen emulates Windows and Linux at the syscall layer, giving security researchers a sharper tool for tracing hostile binaries.
